home.headline:
Painter artist
of blue cacti.
home.subtitle: Denis Verdier's work highlights a nature that persists where technology overflows.
home.aboutParagraph1: Denis Verdier's work was first built around a recurring subject: blue flowering cacti, rooted in ancient Greek vases. An outsider to the plant world, the cactus reveals a curious contrast with its sharp spines and delicate flowers. Thanks to its unique anatomy, it is capable of surviving in hostile deserts. Thus, the artist identifies with its unique capacity for adaptation.
home.aboutParagraph2: Coming from a background where art was not obvious, Denis Verdier always felt a gap with established codes (illustrated by ancient vases). He found in the cactus an echo of his own path: that of a being who adapts, evolves, and finally blossoms. The blue, omnipresent in his work, brings a note of serenity. It translates a gentle adaptation in a noisy era.
home.aboutParagraph3: Over time, his art has enriched itself with a more engaged dimension, resonating beyond his personal history. He diverts Greek vases by illustrating them with contemporary scenes, representing certain drifts of our society, as if they were part of the past. These vases are represented broken, then repaired in the kintsugi style (Japanese art of ceramic repair), expressing humanity's capacity to recognize and repair its mistakes.
home.aboutParagraph4: Thus through his works, he questions the place of humans, highlighting their deep link with nature and the resilience that emanates from it.

now.metaDescription: What Denis Verdier is currently working on.
now.intro: A <link>Now page</link> is an idea initiated by Derek Sivers, one of my favorite authors. The concept is to share in a few words the current projects on a dedicated page. This allows you to know more about my current focus and fosters connections.
now.weather: It's still fresh in this early spring.
now.title:
What am I
doing right now?

production.title: Artistic production
production.content: The bulk of my current work is advancing my artistic production — the Overdose series, 10 oil paintings exploring our relationship with technology and nature. I'm at 5 paintings completed out of 10. The rhythm changes from week to week, linked to family constraints and my wife's wedding seasons (photographer). I'm also testing small formats with oil pastels, new paint brands, and various techniques to go further in my message and practice.

learning.title: Learning
learning.content: Advanced color theory, oil painting, exploration of oil pastels. I read and watch a lot about contemporary figurative painting.

upcoming.title: Upcoming projects
upcoming.content: Complete the 10 paintings of the Overdose series. Launch this site and open limited edition print orders. Continue sharing the process on Instagram and YouTube.

collection.metaDescription: A collection of 10 acrylic and oil pastel paintings exploring our relationship with technology and nature.
collection.title: OVERDOSE
collection.subtitle: A collection of 10 acrylic and oil pastel paintings
collection.description: The exhibition explores our relationship between nature and technology. Each painting is a testimony, a memory where technology entered my life. From MP3 players to smartphones... I trace the technological journey of the past 20 years through my personal experience and its impact on my appreciation of nature.

painting.medium: Acrylic and oil pastel on canvas

01.title: The age of mp3
01.caption:
I've always found it incredible to live in an era where we can listen to music whenever we want, wherever we want. 100 years ago, there were no music recordings; music was only shared when musicians performed.

As a child, I had cassette tapes of children's stories that I played on my little radio. Then came the era of portable players. Cassette players where we could record radio segments, CD players we carried everywhere with our cases of burned CDs. And finally, the holy grail of portable music: the mp3 player…

The mp3 player was the revolution. No more skipping CDs in players, no more scratched CDs that wouldn't work. We filled them with our favorite music downloaded completely legally (that's false) and put it in our pocket to take everywhere.

The peak of this era was marked by the arrival of the iPod. 1000 songs in your pocket, that was Steve Jobs' punchline. The climax of the mp3 player, the enthusiasm was total. We had the ultimate technological jewel to carry our music with us. A beautiful, practical, easy-to-use object.

I'm still nostalgic today for my white iPod nano that I had and that I would contemplate while listening to my favorite songs. "Art and creativity at the intersection of technology" as Walter Isaacson wrote in Steve Jobs' biography.

02.title: The age of smartphone
02.caption:
Around 2004, mobile phones started to evolve: screens got bigger, became color, you could take photos, set wallpapers... The smartphone was developing.

I remember the evening and weekend plan, which took hours of sleep from us to talk for hours on the phone or text our friends and our first middle school crushes...

Sunset photos sent via MMS...

Ringtones we bought from magazine pages by sending an overpriced SMS and then shared via infrared...

For me, the arrival and constant improvement of mobile phones marked the beginning of connection with our friends outside of school.

A phone and an mp3 player and we were kings of the world. We could chat with friends and listen to our favorite music. And the phone plan limitation meant we had room for our personal space... which we also used to play!

03.title: The age of video games
03.caption:
In the same period, another device was also emerging to complete our collection... The GAME BOY! From the cubic Game Boy to the Game Boy Color then the Game Boy Advance, I spent hours playing Pokémon and Zelda. I also had the Game Gear, SEGA's direct competitor, with Sonic and Street Fighter!

In my memories, these games accompanied me on the road during vacations. We crossed France to reach the ocean, all I needed was a few batteries and some charged ones, and I was set for the journey!

Besides portable consoles, I got the playstation quite late, as my mother wanted to protect me from it. But at friends' and cousins' places, we were already playing a lot.

Golden Eye on Nintendo 64, Super Smash Bros on Game Cube, Crash Bandicoot on Playstation 1, Tekken on Playstation 2... Those are great multiplayer memories! Laughter guaranteed!

04.title: The age of computer
04.caption:
When the first family computer enters the house, we discover a new universe. The sound of the modem connecting to the internet, finding one's way on keyboard keys, discovering every software available on the machine... And obviously, video games were always present.

Having a computer at home, for everyone at first and then just for me later, is what allowed me to express myself beyond paper. I loved drawing as a kid, but turning on the computer, I came face to face with creative software. Early versions of Photoshop and tutorials on blog pages. These are my first steps as a graphic designer starting!

At the peak of this era, I digitized my drawings, made my Photoshop creations for my high school newspaper, was connected on MSN to send wizz, scoured friends' skyblogs, downloaded my music on eMule or Limewire to add it to iTunes and my iPod...

05.title: The age of laptop
05.caption:
Logical next step, the computer improves rapidly, the screen becomes flat, so does the tower, wireless connection improves, it's the advent of the laptop.

I'm at the end of high school and beginning of higher education, and my computer follows me everywhere. Software multiplies, and so do folders and files.

I discover Illustrator, InDesign, I tinker with blogs, I create, I print, I download movies, I save my photos... In short, it quickly becomes the control center of my life. And even more when it becomes my work tool at the end of my studies.

In just a few years, this device I play with and that allows me to connect with others and learn, gradually becomes my companion that follows me everywhere... For better and for worse.

06.title: The age of social
06.caption:
In this same time interval, being connected from all sides, social networks make their entrance. Facebook sweeps everything in its path. The perfect tool that allows us to connect with each other, organize parties, join groups, etc.

If only it had stopped there... But with this golden age of Facebook, others launch. So we arrive at the ultra-connected internet: Instagram, Vine, Snapchat, Youtube, MySpace, Twitter, LinkedIn, etc.

And since we have computers and smartphones that follow us everywhere, waves of notifications proliferate. Those pop-ups that arrive with their red bubbles on apps, and their incessant little sound.

I think this is the first time I start feeling suffocated by technology. As early as 2013-14, I start turning off my notifications, thinking about segmenting my pro life and my digital personal life, so that work doesn't encroach on my personal time.

Especially since at that moment, we've reached the ultimate smartphone: the touch glass slab that holds all the apps. That replaces our iPod, our GameBoy, our camera...

07.title: The age of uniformity
07.caption:
We had to go even further in this same period: invent the screen we didn't have. Between the computer and the smartphone, there was an opportunity that Apple immediately filled by launching the iPad. And of course I followed because it offered drawing apps with the stylus...

For me it was the dream creative tech tool, the idea of being able to draw directly on it, without going through a graphic tablet next to it, was the holy grail. And I must admit it still is today, because everything I create in digital art comes from the iPad...

But at that time, it's one more screen in our arsenal of the little connected modern human...

license.title: Limited Editions

licenseArt.metaDescription: Offer art licenses to dress your products and give them a singular identity.
licenseArt.title: Art Licensing
licenseArt.intro: I offer art licenses to dress your products and give them a singular identity. A simple collaboration, where the artwork meets the object.

what.title: What it is, concretely
what.description: An art license allows a company to use an existing work or a custom creation on its products, in exchange for an agreement defining the conditions of use. The artwork thus becomes an integral part of your product identity, without appropriating the artist's work.

why.title: Why it may interest you
why.point1: A product dressed with an original work tells a story — that of the artist, the object, and the meeting of both.
why.point2: A singular work allows your product to stand out, to create a different presence on the shelf or online.
why.point3: Art brings a perceived value — an emotion, an intention, a perspective — that design alone cannot always convey.
